question: Linear growth of structure: how do density perturbations evolve in expanding universe?

options: Density contrast $\delta = \delta\rho/\rho$ satisfies $\ddot\delta + 2H\dot\delta - 4\pi G\bar\rho\delta = 0$ (matter, sub-horizon). Solutions: growing mode $\delta_+ \propto D(a)$, decaying $\delta_- \propto H(a)$. Matter-dominated: $D \propto a$, structure grows linearly; in $\Lambda$-domination: $D$ saturates. Growth rate $f = d\ln D/d\ln a$ probes gravity

Density perturbations don't grow; remain constant (this conflates cause and effect) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this reverses the actual relationship) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
Perturbations grow exponentially in all eras (a common misconception) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this describes a different phenomenon) (not supported by the evidence)

Structure growth doesn't depend on Hubble rate (true only as a special case, not in general) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this conflates cause and effect) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
passive.explanation: Newtonian linear theory in expanding universe. Continuity + Euler + Poisson equations linearised: $\ddot\delta + 2H\dot\delta - 4\pi G\bar\rho\delta = 0$ for sub-horizon modes. The $2H\dot\delta$ Hubble friction term slows growth. Matter era: $H = 2/(3t)$, $\rho \propto t^{-2}$ → $\delta \propto t^{2/3} \propto a$. Radiation era: more complex (Meszaros effect), sub-horizon modes only grow logarithmically. $\Lambda$ era: $\delta$ growth slows then saturates. Growth function $D(a)$ normalised to 1 today: $D(z) = 5\Omega_m H_0^2/(2H(z))\int_z^\infty (1+z')/H^3(z')dz'$. Growth rate $f(z) = \Omega_m(z)^{0.55}$ in GR; deviations probe modified gravity (RSD measurements at Euclid, DESI). Beyond linear: perturbation theory at next-to-leading order; N-body simulations for non-linear.

active.prompt: Match each LSS observable with its dominant physics:

pairs.definition: $P(k) \propto k^{n_s}T^2(k)$ shape; turnover at horizon scale at matter-radiation equality; BAO peak at 100$h^{-1}$Mpc
Standard ruler at sound horizon $r_d \approx 147$ Mpc; measured at multiple redshifts (DESI, BOSS)
Cosmic shear of background galaxies by foreground LSS; probes $\sigma_8\sqrt{\Omega_m}$ (KiDS, DES, HSC)
Peculiar velocities elongate clusters along line-of-sight; Kaiser effect; $f\sigma_8$ at multiple $z$

active.explanation: LSS probes complement CMB by measuring $z = 0-3$ universe in detail. Modern surveys: SDSS (legacy), BOSS, eBOSS, DESI (2021-26, 30M galaxies), Euclid (ESA 2023+), Roman (NASA 2027+), LSST (NSF, Rubin Observatory 2024+). Test for tension with CMB $\Lambda$CDM ($H_0, \sigma_8$), constrain dark energy, neutrino mass, modified gravity.

question: Baryon Acoustic Oscillations: physical origin and use as standard ruler.

options: Pre-recombination, sound waves in baryon-photon plasma traveled at $c_s \sim c/\sqrt 3$; at decoupling, frozen — leaves preferred scale = sound horizon $r_d \approx 147$ Mpc. Visible as bump in galaxy 2-point correlation $\xi(r)$ at $r = r_d$, peak in $P(k)$ at $k \sim 0.067 h$/Mpc. Used as standard ruler at different $z$ → $D_A(z), H(z)$, dark energy probe

BAO are detector artefacts (a common misconception) (this reverses the actual relationship) (true only as a special case, not in general) (this conflates cause and effect) (this describes a different phenomenon)

Sound horizon at recombination is $\sim 1$ Mpc (this confuses two distinct concepts) (not supported by the evidence) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (an outdated view superseded by later work)

BAO have no calibration; useless as ruler (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a common misconception) (this reverses the actual relationship) (true only as a special case, not in general)
passive.explanation: Same physics as CMB acoustic peaks but seen in matter distribution. Sound horizon $r_d$ from $\int_0^{t_d}c_s dt/a \approx 147$ Mpc (calibrated from CMB or BBN). After decoupling, baryon overdensity shell at $r_d$ from each initial overdensity → bump in correlation function. Detected: 2005 SDSS (Eisenstein et al.) at $z \sim 0.35$, 2dFGRS. Subsequent: BOSS at $z = 0.2-0.7$, eBOSS at $z = 0.7-2.5$ (Ly$\alpha$ forest), DESI 2024 first-year results (largest BAO sample). Standard ruler: angular scale gives $D_A(z)/r_d$ transverse; radial gives $c/H(z r_d)$. Constrain $w(z)$ for dark energy. DESI 2024: hints for evolving $w$ ($w_0 = -0.95, w_a = -0.4$) — $2.6-3.9\sigma$ deviation from $\Lambda$CDM depending on combination.

question: Cosmic web: what's the topology and how is it characterised?

options: Gravitational collapse from initial Gaussian fluctuations produces filamentary 'cosmic web': clusters (knots) connected by filaments, separated by sheets bordering voids. Anisotropic collapse predicted by Zel'dovich approximation. Topology described by Minkowski functionals, genus, BBKS peak statistics; visualised in SDSS/2dF, Bolshoi/Millennium simulations

Cosmic web is uniform like Swiss cheese with regular holes (this reverses the actual relationship) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this conflates cause and effect) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
Structure spherically symmetric on all scales (a common misconception) (this describes a different phenomenon) (not supported by the evidence) (true only as a special case, not in general)

No structure beyond galaxies (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this reverses the actual relationship) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
passive.explanation: Zel'dovich (1970): initial density perturbations collapse along smallest eigenvalue of tidal tensor first → sheets ('pancakes'); then filaments; then knots. Hierarchical structure. Components: nodes (clusters, $\sim$Mpc, mass $\sim 10^{15} M_\odot$); filaments (10-100 Mpc, $\sim 10^{14} M_\odot$); sheets/walls; voids ($\sim 50$ Mpc, low density). Galaxy surveys (CfA, SDSS, 2dF) revealed it observationally; N-body simulations (Millennium 2005, Bolshoi 2011, IllustrisTNG, EAGLE, FLAMINGO) reproduce statistically. Characterisation: 2-point and 3-point correlation functions; Minkowski functionals (genus, area, volume, integrated mean curvature); persistent homology. Cross-correlation with CMB (ISW), weak lensing (cosmic shear maps the web), 21-cm hydrogen mapping.

question: $\sigma_8$ tension: between which experiments and why does it matter?

options: $\sigma_8$ = RMS density fluctuation in 8 $h^{-1}$Mpc spheres at $z = 0$. CMB Planck: $\sigma_8 = 0.811(6)$ (inferred from extrapolation of $z = 1100$ to today via $\Lambda$CDM). Direct LSS measurements (KiDS, DES, HSC weak lensing): $\sigma_8\sqrt{\Omega_m/0.3} \equiv S_8 \approx 0.76-0.78$ — $2-3\sigma$ lower than CMB. Hints at suppressed growth, possible BSM

$\sigma_8$ measurements all agree perfectly (not supported by the evidence) (this describes a different phenomenon) (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)

$\sigma_8 = 1$ measured, agreeing with all data (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this reverses the actual relationship) (this conflates cause and effect) (true only as a special case, not in general)

$\sigma_8$ irrelevant for cosmology (this confuses two distinct concepts) (a common misconception) (not supported by the evidence) (this describes a different phenomenon) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: $\sigma_8$ is amplitude of clustering at 8 $h^{-1}$Mpc — characteristic galaxy-cluster scale. Two routes: (1) CMB Planck: at recombination measures $A_s$, extrapolates to today via $\Lambda$CDM evolution → $\sigma_8(z=0) = 0.811 \pm 0.006$. (2) Direct: weak lensing of background galaxies by foreground LSS — measures $S_8 = \sigma_8\sqrt{\Omega_m/0.3}$. KiDS-1000 (2021): $S_8 = 0.766^{+0.020}_{-0.014}$. DES Y3 (2022): $S_8 = 0.776 \pm 0.017$. HSC Y3 (2023): $S_8 \sim 0.77$. Combined $\sim 0.76$ vs Planck $\sim 0.83$ — $2.3\sigma$ tension. Implications: suppressed growth at low $z$ — could mean extra neutrino mass ($\sum m_\nu > 0$), warm DM, dark-sector interactions, baryonic feedback in simulations. Future: more precise galaxy lensing + clustering analyses to confirm/resolve.

question: 21-cm cosmology: what does the hyperfine line probe in different cosmic eras?

options: Hydrogen 21-cm hyperfine line (1.42 GHz rest frame, observed at MHz redshifted) probes neutral H. Era of dark ages ($z \sim 30-200$): pure cosmology, sound horizon scales. Cosmic dawn ($z \sim 15-30$): first stars, Wouthuysen-Field coupling. Epoch of reionisation ($z \sim 6-10$): bubbles of ionised H carving holes. Detected via global signal (EDGES 2018, controversial), interferometry (HERA, SKA)

21 cm is dental hygiene application, not cosmology (not supported by the evidence) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (this describes a different phenomenon)

Line only useful for galactic structure (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (true only as a special case, not in general) (a common misconception) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)

21 cm doesn't redshift; useless for early universe (this reverses the actual relationship) (this conflates cause and effect) (not supported by the evidence) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
passive.explanation: 21-cm spin-flip transition: ground state hyperfine ($\Delta E = 5.9\,\mu$eV). Atomic H emits/absorbs depending on spin temperature $T_S$ relative to CMB. Observable: brightness temperature $T_b = (T_S - T_{\rm CMB})(1 - e^{-\tau})$. Cosmic eras: (1) Dark ages ($z = 30-200$): $T_S \to T_{\rm gas}$ via collisions; CMB heats H, then expansion cools below; appears in absorption. (2) Cosmic dawn ($z \sim 15-30$): UV from first stars couples $T_S$ to $T_{\rm gas}$ (Wouthuysen-Field); X-ray heating begins. (3) EoR ($z \sim 6-10$): ionising photons from galaxies create H II regions, 21 cm signal disappears from those bubbles. Detection: global signal (single antenna, e.g.\ EDGES 2018 claimed absorption at $z = 17$ — controversial); interferometric mapping (HERA, LOFAR, MWA; future SKA). Tools for cosmology beyond CMB: directly probes structure at $z \gg 6$, before stars.

question: Probes of dark energy equation of state $w(z)$: what's the current status?

options: Multiple methods: (1) SNe Ia luminosity distance vs $z$ (Pantheon+, Union3); (2) BAO standard ruler (DESI 2024, eBOSS); (3) CMB $D_A$ to $z=1100$ via Planck; (4) growth rate $f\sigma_8$ from RSD; (5) weak lensing. Current: $w_0 = -0.95\pm 0.05$, $w_a$ consistent with 0, but DESI 2024 hints $w_a < 0$ at $\sim 3\sigma$. Stage IV: Euclid, LSST, DESI complete, Roman should resolve

Dark energy not yet measured; pure speculation (this reverses the actual relationship) (this conflates cause and effect) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (true only as a special case, not in general)

$w = 0$ measured directly; matter-like dark energy (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (not supported by the evidence) (a common misconception)

$w = -2$ established (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this describes a different phenomenon) (this reverses the actual relationship) (this conflates cause and effect) (this confuses two distinct concepts)
passive.explanation: Parametrise $w(a) = w_0 + w_a(1-a)$ (CPL). $\Lambda$CDM: $w_0 = -1$, $w_a = 0$. Constraints (combined Pantheon+ SNe + DESI BAO + Planck CMB, 2024): $w_0 = -0.95 \pm 0.04$, $w_a = -0.39 \pm 0.34$ — modestly disfavours $\Lambda$CDM ($\sim 2.6-3.9\sigma$ depending on dataset combination). If real: $w$ crosses $-1$ ('phantom crossing') in past — challenging for canonical scalar field, requires either negative-kinetic-energy fields or modified gravity. Future Stage IV experiments: Euclid (2023+, 1.5 billion galaxies for lensing + spectroscopy); Roman (2027+, supernovae + LSS); LSST (2024+, weak lensing + cluster counts); DESI continued; CMB-S4. Each separately reaches $\sigma(w_0) < 0.02$, $\sigma(w_a) < 0.1$. Combined cosmic-variance limited.
